PART II - POLICY ADMINISTRATION
GC 6005  Section C - Policy Termination, Page 1
Section C - Policy Termination
Article 1 - Failure to Pay Premium
This Group Policy will terminate at the end of the Grace Period if total premium due has not
been received by The Principal before the end of the Grace Period.  Failure by the Policyholder
to pay the premium within the Grace Period will be deemed notice by the Policyholder to The
Principal to discontinue this Group Policy at the end of the Grace Period.
Article 2 - Termination Rights of the Policyholder
The Policyholder may terminate this Group Policy effective on the day before any premium due
date by giving Written notice to The Principal prior to that premium due date.  The
Policyholder's issuance of a stop- payment order for any amounts used to pay premiums for the
Policyholder's coverage will be considered Written notice from the Policyholder.
Article 3 - Termination Rights of The Principal
The Principal may nonrenew or terminate this Group Policy by giving the Policyholder 31 days
advance notice in Writing, if the Policyholder:
a. ceases to be actively engaged in business for profit within the meaning of the Internal
Revenue Code, o

PART II - POLICY ADMINISTRATION
GC 6004 Section B - Premiums, Page 1
Section B - Premiums
Article 1 - Payment Responsibility; Due Dates; Grace Period
The Policyholder is responsible for collection and payment of all premiums due while this Group
Policy is in force.  Payments must be sent to the home office of The Principal in Des Moines,
Iowa.
The first premium is due on the Date of Issue of this Group Policy.  Each premium thereafter
will be due on the first of each Insurance Month.  Except for the first premium, a Grace Period of
31 days will be allowed for payment of premium.  "Grace Period" means the first 31- day period
following a premium due date.  The Group Policy will remain in fo rce until the end of the Grace
Period, unless the Group Policy has been terminated by notice as described in PART II, Section
C.  The Policyholder will be liable for payment of the premium for the time this Group Policy
remains in force during the Grace Period.
Article 2 - Premium Rates
The premium rate(s) for each Member insured for Life Insurance will be:
a. Member Life Insurance
$0.210 for each $1,000 of insurance in force.

1.5.1 Research Aim
The primary aim of this research is to develop and rigorously evaluate a bias-aware multimodal
deep learning framework for pancreatic cancer detection that integrates CT imaging and urinary
biomarker data, while systematically analyzing the impact of dataset bias, modality dominance,
and alignment constraints on model performance.

6.1 Introduction
This final chapter pulls together the main findings, assesses how well the three research
objectives were met, answers the five research questions from Chapter 1, and looks at what the
work contributes and where it falls short. No new experimental results are presented here; the
aim is to synthesize, reflect, and point forward.
The study set out to develop and rigorously evaluate a bias -aware multimodal deep learning
framework for pancreatic cancer detection, combining CT imaging with urinary biomarker data
while paying close attention to dataset bias, modality dominance, and the  constraints imposed
by working with unmatched patient cohorts. The pipeline spanned data acquisition and
preprocessing, systematic bias detection and mitigation, unimodal model development for both
CT and biomarkers, multimodal fusion at decision and feature levels, and transparent evaluation
with negative controls and multi-seed validation.

1.5.2 Research Objectives
To achieve this aim, three specific research objectives were defined:
● Establish a reliable and bias -aware data foundation for pancreatic cancer detection by
preparing CT imaging and biomarker datasets, enforcing patient -level leakage control,
and ensuring reproducible data readiness for downstream analysis.
● Develop and evaluate unimodal diagnostic models for CT imaging and urinary
biomarkers to quantify the independent predictive value and limitations of each
modality under transparent  evaluation.
● Investigate whether multimodal fusion provides meaningful value beyond unimodal
baselines under synthetic pairing constraints, while explicitly analyzing modality
dominance, residual bias effects, and robustness across controlled validation settings.
Together, these objectives emphasize clinical relevance, methodological validity, and
transparent reporting rather than metric-driven optimization alone. Detailed procedures used to
carry out these objectives are provided in Chapter 3, with implementation specific details
presented in Chapter 4.

The preprocessing script indexed 24,627 slices and retained 23,319 slices after script -level

the notebook pipeline.

3.4.2 Extreme Intensity Standardization
The extreme intensity standardization procedure was developed after two earlier attempts:
histogram matching and forced global standardization, both failed to solve the problem. The
histogram matching approach attempted to align the cumulative distribution  function of each
image to a reference distribution computed from the control dataset, but failed to eliminate bias

4.4.1 CT Classification Model (ResNet50)
Architecture and Initialization: The CT classification model was built on ResNet50 (He et al.,
2016) with weights pretrained on ImageNet (IMAGENET1K_V2). The architecture comprises
a 50-layer residual network organized into four convolutional stages with bottleneck blocks,
followed by global average pooling that produces a 2048 -dimensional embedding vector. The
original 1000-unit ImageNet classification head was replaced with a two -unit fully connected
layer for binary cancer versus control classification. Full fine-tuning was employed, with all
convolutional and batch normalization layers set as trainable, allowing the network to adapt its
learned representations from the natural image domain to abdominal CT imaging. The complete

candidates.text:
3.6.1 CT Classification Model
ResNet50 (He et al., 2016)  was selected as the base architecture for CT image classification.
The architecture comprises a 50 -layer residual network organized into four stages with
bottleneck blocks containing 1×1, 3×3, and 1×1 convolutional layers connected by skip
connections. Th e skip connection design, adding the input of a block directly to its output
mitigates vanishing gradients and enables effective training of deeper networks. Global average
pooling produces a 2048-dimensional embedding vector, which is passed to a fully connected
classification head with two output units for binary cancer versus control classification.
The model was initialized with weights pretrained on ImageNet (IMAGENET1K_V2),
providing general -purpose low -level feature extractors for edges, textures, and shapes. Full
network fine-tuning was employed, with all layers set as trainable. While alternative strategies
such as freezing the backbone or progressive unfreezing were considered, full fine -tuning was
adopted to allow the model maximum capacity to adapt its learned representations to the
substantially different domain of abdominal CT imaging.

The proposed model consists of two parallel deep learning branches, one for each modality:
• Imaging Pathway: A pretrained ResNet50 CNN will be used for feature extraction ,
lower-level layers pretrained on ImageNet will be retained, while higher layers will be
fine-tuned on the CT dataset. The output will be flattened and passed through a dense
layer that results in a 256D embedding.
• Biomarker Pathway:  A multilayer perceptron (MLP) will process the normalized
biomarker vector through three dense layers (128, 64, 32 units) with ReLU activations.
Dropout (0.3) and batch normalization will be performed to reduce overfitting. The final
output will be a 32D embedding.

The biomarker classification model was implemented as a two-layer MLP (7 → 64 → 32 →

3.6.2 Biomarker Classification Model
A multi -layer perceptron (MLP) was employed for biomarker classification, reflecting the
relatively low dimensionality of the input (seven features) and the modest dataset size (590
samples). The architecture comprises an input layer accepting seven features, two hidden layers
with 64 and 32 neurons respectively, each followed by batch normalization, ReLU activation,
and dropout (p = 0.3), and a two-unit output layer for binary classification.
A shallow architecture was deliberately chosen to reduce overfitting risk on the modest tabular
dataset. Dropout regularization at 30% was used in both hidden layers. The model was trained
with AdamW (learning rate 1e -3, weight decay 1e -4), batch size 32, cross-entropy loss with
class weighting computed from the training -class ratio, and 50 training epochs, with best -
checkpoint selection by validation AUC.

5.4.2 Comparison with Original Study
The biomarker panel was originally characterized by (Debernardi et al., 2020) , who reported
AUC values of 0.936 for distinguishing PDAC from healthy controls using a logistic regression
model on a similar feature set. The MLP model developed in this study achieved a comparable
AUC of 0.944, providing independent validation of the d iagnostic utility of the urinary
biomarker panel. The slight improvement may be attributable to the MLP’s capacity to model
nonlinear feature interactions, though the difference i s within the range of stochastic variation
given the sample size.
This replication is methodologically significant. It demonstrates that the biomarker signal is
robust to different modelling approaches (logistic regression versus neural network), different
preprocessing pipelines, and different train/test partitioning st rategies. The consistency of the
AUC across these variations supports the biological validity of the biomarker panel for PDAC
detection.

candidates.text:
5.5 Multimodal Fusion Results
The multimodal fusion experiments aimed to demonstrate the methodological framework for
combining CT imaging and biomarker modalities, rather than to claim externally validated
clinical performance. As noted in Chapter 4, the CT and biomarker datasets were  drawn from
different patient cohorts, necessitating synthetic pairing through label -matched random
assignment. Furthermore, the CT model retains residual domain signal despite bias mitigation.
Given these constraints, the fusion results should be read as a methodological proof-of-concept
rather than evidence of clinical performance.
To ensure scientific rigor, all fusion experiments incorporated two verification mechanisms: (a)
multi-seed repetition across five random seeds (seeds 0 –4) to assess stability, and (b) label -
mismatch control experiments in which synthetic pairs were deliberately created with incorrect
label alignment, providing a baseline against which to gauge the contribution of genuine label
information.

18
Synthetic pairing severs any genuine patient -level correspondence between modalities. In a
clinically deployed system, a patient’s CT scan and their biomarker profile would reflect the
same underlying disease state, creating meaningful cross -modal correlations. With synthetic
pairing, these correlations do not exist. Consequently, feature -level fusion which attempts to
learn joint representations operates on concatenated embeddings that share no underlying
patient identity. Decision -level fusion, which mere ly averages independent predictions, is
somewhat less affected by this limitation, as it does not assume cross -modal feature
interactions.
This study explicitly tests whether synthetic pairing produces genuine fusion benefit or merely
reflects the dominance of the stronger individual modality. This question is rarely addressed in
the multimodal medical imaging literature, where fusion improve ments are often reported
without rigorous controls for modality dominance.

6.6.4 Prospective Validation and Clinical Integration
Ultimately, the real test is whether the system works in a prospective clinical setting. Future
work should include reader comparison studies in which the model’s outputs are evaluated
alongside radiologist assessments on unseen cases, integration with cli nical decision-support
workflows to assess practical utility and clinician acceptance, and regulatory pathway
engagement for any component that demonstrates robust prospective performance. The
biomarker model, given its strong and reproducible performance on an independently validated
feature set and its non-invasive nature, may be the most immediately translatable component of
the framework for pilot screening studies in high-risk populations.

Research results
In the binary classifiers, the performance of plain, arterial and venous phase showed
no difference.  Considering  that plain phase had relatively  same sensitivity,  easier
access, and lower radiation compared with arterial phase and venous phase, it is more
sufficient for the binary classifier. Its accuracy on plain scans was 95.47%, sensitivity
was 91.58%, and specificity was 98.27%. In the ternary classifier, the arterial phase had
the highest  sensitivity  in detecting  cancer in the head of the pancreas  among three
phases, but it achieved only moderate performances.
Research conclusions
In this study, we developed a deep learning-based, computer-aided pancreatic ductal
adenocarcinoma  classifier  trained  on medium-sized  CT images.  It was suitable  for
screening purposes in pancreatic cancer detection.
Research perspectives

The need for multimodal data fusion in oncology
Despite huge investments in cancer research and improved diagnosis and treatments, cancer
prognosis is still bleak. Predictive models based on single modalities offer a limited view
of disease heterogeneity and might not provide sufficient information to stratify patients
and capture the full range of events that take place in response to treatments41, 42. For
example, although immunotherapeutic methods like antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs) and
adoptive cell therapy (ACT) (e.g. T-cell receptor (TCR) and chimeric antigen receptor
T-cell (CAR-T) therapy) have shown to be very promising, response rates vary dramatically
depending on the tumour subtype43 and the TME44. Various TME elements play a role in
tumour development, but also in therapeutic response. Furthermore, the cellular composition
of the TME dynamically evolves with tumour progression and in response to anticancer
treatments45, 46. The increasing application of immunotherapy underlines the need for (i) a
deeper understanding of the TME and (ii) multimodal approaches that allow longitudinal
TME monitoring during disease progression and therapeutic intervention47.

amounts of data and data sparsity and scarcity both present serious challenges, especially for
biomedical data. In clinical practice there are often different types of data missing between
patients as not all patients might have all modalities due to cost, insurance coverage,

understanding of cancer3, 4, but also paves the way for precision medicine which promises

The age of precision medicine demands powerful computational techniques to handle high-

Medical images, whether 2D in histopathology or 3D in radiology, contain dense

The need for multimodal data fusion in oncology
Despite huge investments in cancer research and improved diagnosis and treatments, cancer
prognosis is still bleak. Predictive models based on single modalities offer a limited view
of disease heterogeneity and might not provide sufficient information to stratify patients
and capture the full range of events that take place in response to treatments41, 42. For
example, although immunotherapeutic methods like antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs) and
adoptive cell therapy (ACT) (e.g. T-cell receptor (TCR) and chimeric antigen receptor
T-cell (CAR-T) therapy) have shown to be very promising, response rates vary dramatically
depending on the tumour subtype43 and the TME44. Various TME elements play a role in
tumour development, but also in therapeutic response. Furthermore, the cellular composition
of the TME dynamically evolves with tumour progression and in response to anticancer
treatments45, 46. The increasing application of immunotherapy underlines the need for (i) a
deeper understanding of the TME and (ii) multimodal approaches that allow longitudinal
TME monitoring during disease progression and therapeutic intervention47.

Baselines.We evaluate our model against state-of-the-art baselines for both dis-
ease classification and report generation tasks. For disease classification, we com-
pare with: PCAM [31], evaluated using both CheXpert-pretrained and MIMIC-

tion, we compare against R2GenCMN [5], which uses cross-modal memory net-
works for visual-textual integration, and ChatCAD [27].
Implementation Details.We prompt GPT-4 to query the diagnostic criteria.
We use the pretrained weights of BioViL CLIP Resnet-50 [3]. Our model is
implemented with the Bio-ViL specialized. We finetune the model by optimizing
only the visual encoder, visual concept tokens, and the final linear layer with
AdamW optimizer, while keeping the text encoder fixed. All experiments are
conducted using PyTorch with Nvidia RTX 8000 GPUs.
